title: Slatted Four-Post Farmhouse Bed (Queen)
summary: A solid 4x4-post queen bed with slatted 1x12 headboard and footboard panels, joined with pocket screws. Heavy, simple, and built to last.
description:
This is a queen bed with real presence: four 6-foot 4x4 posts, a headboard and footboard framed in 2x6 and 2x10, and vertical 1x12 slats filling the panels. It's all pocket-hole joinery and screws — no mortises — but the mass of the lumber makes it feel like a much fussier build than it is.

Work the pocket holes in two passes to save yourself constant jig resetting: drill every 3/4" pocket hole (all the 1x12 slats) first, then switch the jig to 1-1/2" stock and do all the 2x6 and 2x10 holes. The slats attach to the top and bottom rails with a 3/8" reveal on each side, which keeps the spacing even and hides the exact panel width — the cut list trims the headboard slats to 23-3/4" purely to conserve wood, and the plan notes this doesn't affect strength.

The side rails attach to the posts with either bed-rail brackets (which let the bed knock down for moving) or pocket screws (permanent but simpler). Brackets are worth it on a bed this heavy. Cleats run along the inside of the rails to carry a slat system; the plan leaves the actual mattress slats and center support to you, since those depend on your mattress and whether you want a center leg. Use un-treated 4x4s and let the wood be the finish, or paint.

tools.note: Every joint on this bed is a pocket screw. Get a jig that handles 1-1/2" stock.
For crosscutting the 4x4 posts and long 2x stock.

materials.name: 4x4, 8 ft, untreated
materials.note: Posts, cut to 72". Untreated — this is indoor furniture.

materials.name: Bed-rail brackets
materials.note: Optional but recommended — lets the bed knock down for moving.

cutList.note: Headboard top and bottom, plus footboard top.
Trimmed to 23-3/4" to conserve wood; does not affect strength.
Optional — quantity and spacing depend on your mattress.

steps.title: Drill all the pocket holes in two passes
steps.body: Drill three 3/4" pocket holes on each end of every 1x12 slat first — that's all the 3/4" holes on the whole bed, so you set the jig once. Then reset the jig for 1-1/2" stock and drill the 2x6 headboard rails (two per end) and the footboard bottom panel (three). Doing it in two batches instead of switching back and forth saves a surprising amount of fiddling.

steps.title: Assemble the headboard panel and attach it to the posts
steps.body:
Attach the slats to the top and bottom 2x6 rails with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue, leaving a 3/8" reveal on each side of every 1x12 where it meets the 2x6. Even reveals are what make a slatted panel look intentional rather than approximate.

Attach the finished headboard panel to two of the posts. If you trimmed the slats to 23-3/4" as in the cut list, set the bottom rail 28-1/4" up from the bottom of the posts.

steps.title: Build the footboard
steps.body: Repeat the process for the footboard: slats between the top 2x6 rail and the 2x10 bottom panel, 3/8" reveals, attached to the two shorter-exposed posts. The footboard is lower than the headboard, so the panel sits proportionally down the posts.

steps.title: Attach the side rails to the posts
steps.body: Join the 2x10 side rails to the head- and footboard posts with bed-rail brackets (recommended — the bed comes apart for moving) or with pocket screws for a permanent build. Brackets earn their cost on a bed this heavy the first time you have to move it through a doorway.

steps.title: Add cleats and mattress support
steps.body:
Glue and screw the 2x4 cleats to the inside of the side rails with 2-1/2" screws roughly every 8", positioned so the mattress slats sit at the right height for your mattress and box spring (or slat system).

Lay the 2x4 mattress slats across the cleats. If your mattress needs it, run a center support the length of the bed with a leg down to the floor — a queen span benefits from one. Sand and finish.

images.alt: Queen four-post farmhouse bed with slatted headboard and footboard panels
